one_line_takeaway: SILENTDRIFT shows that action-chunking + delta-pose VLA architectures have an intrinsic open-loop blind spot that lets a black-box, 2%-poisoning-rate backdoor produce smooth, C2-continuous, kinematically undetectable drift attacks achieving ~93% attack success while preserving ~95% clean-task performance on LIBERO.
key_finding: On the main results table, averaged across the four LIBERO suites: VLA-Adapter baseline (clean model) SR = 96.6%, backdoored model CTSR = 95.3% and ASR = 93.2%; pi-0 baseline SR = 93.2%, backdoored model CTSR = 92.4% and ASR = 92.7% -- i.e. at a 2% poisoning rate the attack degrades triggered-task success by over 90% while clean-task performance stays within ~1-2 points of the unpoisoned baseline. Ablations show: (a) chunk size K=1 (closed-loop) yields only 56.1% ASR vs 82.5%/95.6%/96.2% ASR at K=4/8/16, confirming the open-loop drift-accumulation mechanism is the root cause and that ASR grows with chunk size (consistent with the paper's linear-drift proof); (b) activation distance of 0.05m gives only 18.2% ASR (too little accumulation window) vs. 95.6-97.1% ASR at 0.15/0.30/0.60m, validating the keyframe/'point of no return' design; (c) poison ratio of 1% causes ASR to drop to 68.7% (some tasks get zero poisoned episodes) while 2% already saturates ASR near 95.6%, with 4% giving only marginal further gains; (d) Smootherstep achieves ASR comparable to a naive Constant offset and to Smoothstep (94.8-96.6% range), showing the attack's kinematic stealth does not cost potency; (e) trigger size/transparency/color sensitivity study (Table 2) shows the attack is largely invariant to trigger color and size (94.2-96.3% ASR range) with a mild positive correlation between trigger visibility and ASR.
core_contribution: The paper identifies a previously unexploited architectural vulnerability in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robot control models: the combination of action chunking (predicting K future actions per inference step) and delta-pose action representations creates an 'intra-chunk visual open-loop' -- during execution of a K-step chunk the robot integrates predicted delta actions without intermediate visual feedback, so even tiny per-step perturbations accumulate linearly (the paper proves ||E_accum|| = sum of injected deltas over the chunk, e.g. a 1mm per-step drift compounds to a 5cm deviation over a K=50 chunk). Building on this, the authors propose SILENTDRIFT, a black-box data-poisoning backdoor attack with two mechanisms: (1) perturbations shaped by the Smootherstep quintic polynomial S(tau)=6tau^5-15tau^4+10tau^3, which is proven (Appendix A.1.2) to have zero velocity and acceleration at both trajectory-window boundaries (C2 continuity), so the injected drift is kinematically indistinguishable from natural motion and evades velocity/acceleration/jerk-based anomaly detectors; and (2) a 'keyframe attack strategy' that activates the trigger and perturbation only when the end-effector nears the target object (distance < a threshold d_th, default 0.15m), poisoning only the brief 'approach phase' rather than the whole trajectory, which both minimizes the visual/statistical footprint of the poison and times the drift injection at the 'point of no return' where the robot commits to a chunk it can no longer visually correct.
threat_model: Black-box data-poisoning backdoor attacker who can inject a small number of poisoned demonstration trajectories into the training/fine-tuning dataset of a VLA policy (no access to model weights, architecture, or gradients required). The attacker embeds a visual trigger (a red circular patch, radius 5px, alpha=1.0 in the paper's default config) into the scene image and, only when the trigger is present and the end-effector is within a distance threshold of the target object, adds a Smootherstep-shaped delta-pose perturbation (magnitude 0.3m, direction d) to the ground-truth actions for that segment. At deployment/inference time, the same visual trigger causes the fine-tuned VLA to reproduce the smooth drift, causing a 'near-miss' task failure that looks like a natural motion imprecision rather than an obvious backdoor artifact (wrong object grabbed, premature gripper release, etc., as in prior VLA backdoor work).
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section frames this as dual-use research: the same framework used to expose the vulnerability could be used by real attackers to compromise safety-critical robots, and the authors acknowledge risk of misuse. Beyond that self-disclosed limitation: all results are in the LIBERO simulator only, with no real-robot / physical-hardware validation; the attack and defenses are evaluated on only two VLA architectures (VLA-Adapter, pi-0) and one task family (pick-and-place style manipulation), so generalization to other embodiments, sensors, or task types (e.g. locomotion, bimanual, deformable-object manipulation) is untested; the 'Adaptive Chunk Truncation' defense the authors propose in the appendix is explicitly conceptual/not implemented -- they state training the safety monitor is data-intensive, adds real-time latency, and has an unresolved sensitivity/false-positive tuning problem; the only empirically validated defense (forcing chunk size K=1) is shown by the authors' own ablation to only partially reduce ASR (to 56.1%, not to 0) while being impractical due to reintroduced latency.

datasets_used: LIBERO benchmark (LIBERO-Spatial, LIBERO-Object, LIBERO-Goal, LIBERO-10/Long; 10 tasks per suite)
quantitative_results: VLA-Adapter: Baseline SR 96.6% avg (Spatial 99.8, Object 98.4, Goal 96.2, Long 91.8); backdoored CTSR 95.3% avg (99.8, 97.4, 95.2, 88.8); ASR 93.2% avg (95.6, 97.1, 88.7, 91.4)
pi-0: Baseline SR 93.2% avg (Spatial 96.2, Object 97.8, Goal 94.6, Long 84.2); backdoored CTSR 92.4% avg (95.8, 97.0, 93.2, 83.6); ASR 92.7% avg (95.4, 96.9, 88.2, 90.1)
Chunk-size ablation: K=1 CTSR 92.4%/ASR 56.1%; K=4 CTSR 99.2%/ASR 82.5%; K=8 CTSR 99.8%/ASR 95.6%; K=16 CTSR 98.6%/ASR 96.2%
Trigger-activation-distance ablation: 0.05m ASR 18.2%; 0.15m ASR 95.6%; 0.30m ASR 96.7%; 0.60m ASR 97.1%
Perturbation-function ablation: Constant CTSR 99.4%/ASR 96.6%; Smoothstep CTSR 98.2%/ASR 94.8%; Smootherstep (ours) CTSR 99.8%/ASR 95.6%

Trigger-property sensitivity (Table 2): patch radius 3px CTSR 100.0%/ASR 94.2%, 5px CTSR 99.8%/ASR 95.6%, 10px CTSR 99.8%/ASR 96.3%; transparency alpha=0.3 ASR 86.7%, alpha=0.5 ASR 91.2%, alpha=1.0 ASR 95.6%; color Red ASR 95.6%, Blue ASR 95.2%, Green ASR 96.0%

Default attack config: poisoning ratio 2% (1 poisoned episode/task), trigger patch radius 5px alpha=1.0, Smootherstep drift magnitude 0.3m, activation distance threshold 0.15m, fine-tuning up to 15,000 steps on 6x RTX A6000 GPUs
